inline_comments.message: Wouldn't look nicer if reduce those three conditionals into one with alternative operator?

I thought so at first and tried it:

 return p.Service.Tunnel != "ssl" && !strings.HasPrefix(p.State.State, "closed") && !strings.HasPrefix(p.State.State, "filtered")

I also went through:

1. condition inversion

 return !(p.Service.Tunnel == "ssl" || strings.HasPrefix(p.State.State, "closed") || strings.HasPrefix(p.State.State, "filtered"))

2. single condition statement

 if p.Service.Tunnel == "ssl" || strings.HasPrefix(p.State.State, "closed") || strings.HasPrefix(p.State.State, "filtered") {
 	return false
 }
 return true

3. switch statement

 switch {
 case p.Service.Tunnel == "ssl":
 	return false
 case strings.HasPrefix(p.State.State, "closed"):
 	return false
 case strings.HasPrefix(p.State.State, "filtered"):
 	return false
 default:
 	return true
 }

Conditional chain seemed to be idiomatic Go: readable, returns early, easy to write tests (I'm on it) and to check test coverage
